perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means, in respect to each Party: (a) any information in which such Party claims a proprietary and/or confidential interest; (b) all confidential matters of such Party including, without limitation, technical kn ow-how, trade secrets, technical data, new product strategy, new products or processes, analysis, compilations, concepts, technical processes, formulae, recipes, specifications, inventions, research projects, customer lists, pricing, pricing policies, operational methods, financial information, marketing information and other business affairs; (c) any information of a confidential nature concerning such Party’s customers, suppliers or employees; and (d) any information such Party has received from othe rs which they are obliged to treat as proprietary and/or confidential .
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means, in respect to each Party: (a) any information in which such Party claims a proprietary and/or confidential interest; (b) all confidential matters of such Party excluding trade secrets, technical data, new product strategy, new products or processes, analysis, compilations, concepts, technical processes, formulae, recipes, specifications, inventions, research projects, customer lists, pricing, pricing policies, operational methods, financial information, marketing information and other business affairs; (c) any information of a confidential nature concerning such Party’s customers, suppliers or employees; and (d) any information such Party has received from othe rs which they are obliged to treat as proprietary and/or confidential .
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract creates a conflict with standard trade secret protections. Trade secrets are generally considered part of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By excluding trade secrets from the definition of confidential information, the contract weakens protection for information that would otherwise be protected under the UTSA.
perturbation.justification: The changed contract text explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The Perturbation Explanation states that this exclusion creates a conflict with standard trade secret protections, as trade secrets are generally considered part of confidential information. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the UTSA defines a trade secret as information deriving independent economic value from not being generally known, and excluding it weakens its protection. Although there are no scraped snippets, the alteration in the contract, combined with the explanations provided, sufficiently demonstrates that the change contradicts established legal understanding regarding the confidentiality of trade secrets, establishing a legal contradiction by misaligning terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information i n strict confidence and take all reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree o f care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall (i) maintain the Confidential Information i n reasonable confidence and take commercially reasonable steps to prevent its disclosure to third parties; (ii) use at least the same degree of care as Recipient uses in maintaining the secrecy of its own Confidential Information (but no less than a reasonable degree o f care); and (iii) prevent the removal of any proprietary, confidential or copyright notices placed on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable confidence' lowers the standard of care required for protecting confidential information. This introduces ambiguity and could be interpreted as a lesser obligation than what is typically expected for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act makes it a federal crime to misappropriate a trade secret. By lowering the standard of care, the contract potentially conflicts with the obligation to protect trade secrets from misappropriation as required to avoid violations of the EEA.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'strict confidence' to 'reasonable confidence' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) regarding trade secret misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1832, addresses 'Theft of trade secrets' and mentions 'intent to convert a trade secret...to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof'. By lowering the standard of care, the modified contract increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, potentially contradicting the obligations under the EEA to protect those trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing obligations of all Parties shall continue for a period of two (2) years from the date of last disclosure of Confidential Information by either party, unless a specific portion of the Confidential FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 3 Information disclosed becomes generally known to the public before the expiration of such two (2) year period through no fault of the Receiving Party . However, with respect to trade secrets, such obligations will survive for so long as such Confidential Information constitutes a trade secret under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing obligations of all Parties shall continue for a period of two (2) years from the date of last disclosure of Confidential Information by either party, unless a specific portion of the Confidential FSMA FSMA Approved M utual Confidentiality 2013.05.24 3 Information disclosed becomes generally known to the public before the expiration of such two (2) year period through no fault of the Receiving Party . However, with respect to trade secrets, such obligations will only survive for an additional two years beyond the initial two year period.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the survival of obligations for trade secrets to a fixed term of four years, even if the information remains a trade secret under the UTSA, directly contradicts the UTSA's provision for indefinite protection as long as the information maintains its trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA provides for the protection of trade secrets as long as they remain trade secrets. By limiting the protection period for trade secrets to four years, the modified text directly contradicts the UTSA's provision for ongoing protection.

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the protection of trade secrets to a fixed four-year period, directly contradicting the principle that trade secret protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While there are no law snippets available to confirm this directly, the change fundamentally alters the duration of trade secret protection in a way that undermines its purpose.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNE D BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF THE PRINCIPAL PLACE OF BUSINESS OF DISTRIBUTOR AS SET FORTH IN THE FIRST PARAGRAPH ABOVE (REGARDLESS OF THE LAWS THAT MIGHT OTHERWISE GOVERN UNDER APPLICABLE PRINCIPLES OF CONFLICTS OF LAW) AS TO ALL MATTERS, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO MATTERS OF VALIDITY, CONSTRUCTION, EFFECT, PERFORMANCE AND REMEDY.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNE D BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF [Hypothetical Foreign Country] (REGARDLESS OF THE LAWS THAT MIGHT OTHERWISE GOVERN UNDER APPLICABLE PRINCIPLES OF CONFLICTS OF LAW) AS TO ALL MATTERS, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO MATTERS OF VALIDITY, CONSTRUCTION, EFFECT, PERFORMANCE AND REMEDY.
perturbation.explanation: If the hypothetical foreign country has laws that do not recognize or adequately protect trade secrets, applying that law to this agreement would weaken the protections afforded to confidential information and trade secrets under US law, thereby contradicting the intention to protect such information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA creates a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By choosing a jurisdiction with potentially weaker trade secret laws, the contract could limit the remedies available under the DTSA, effectively contradicting the intent and protections of the federal law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law with that of a "Hypothetical Foreign Country." The Law Explanation states this could weaken trade secret law protections, potentially contradicting the intent and protections of federal law, like the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation. By selecting a jurisdiction with weaker trade secret laws, the modified contract might limit remedies available under the DTSA, creating a legal contradiction by undermining the protection offered by US law.

perturbation.original_text: In case any one or more of the provisions contained herein shall, for any reason, be held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable in any respect, such inval idity, illegality or unenforceability shall not affect any other provisions of this Agreement, and this Agreement shall be construed and enforced as if such invalid, illegal or unenforceable provision(s) had never been contained herein, provided that such invalid, illegal or unenforceable provision(s) shall first be curtailed, reformed, limited or eliminated to the extent necessary to remove such invalidity, illegality or unenforceability with respect to the applicable law as it shall then be applied.
perturbation.changed_text: In case any one or more of the provisions contained herein shall, for any reason, be held to be invalid, illegal or unenforceable in any respect, such inval idity, illegality or unenforceability shall automatically terminate the entire Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the severability clause with a clause that automatically terminates the entire agreement if any provision is deemed invalid introduces significant risk. If a key provision related to trade secret protection is found unenforceable due to conflict with state or federal law, the entire agreement, including all other protections, would be nullified.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles of Severability
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts outlines general principles of severability, indicating that courts generally prefer to sever invalid provisions rather than invalidate the entire agreement, unless the invalid provision is integral to the agreement's purpose. The modified text's automatic termination clause contradicts this principle and creates unnecessary uncertainty in enforcing the agreement's core provisions, including trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a severability clause, which is a standard legal practice that allows the rest of the contract to remain in effect even if one provision is deemed invalid. The changed text replaces this with a clause that automatically terminates the entire agreement if any provision is invalid. The Law Explanation states that courts generally prefer to sever invalid provisions rather than invalidate the entire agreement, unless the invalid provision is integral to the agreement's purpose. Scraped Law Snippet 2 says, "Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute." The changed text contradicts the standard legal practice of severability, potentially voiding the entire agreement due to a single invalid provision. Therefore, the modified contract introduces a significant legal contradiction by not adhering to principles favoring the preservation of contracts.
